task_completion.description: The target landscape image is opened in an image editing application (e.g., GIMP).
task_completion.visual_evidence: The screenshot shows a landscape photograph loaded into the main canvas of an image editor.

task_completion.description: The Solarize filter or effect tool is accessed.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A menu item for 'Solarize' is selected, or a 'Solarize' dialog box/settings panel is visible on the screen.

task_completion.description: The solarization effect is visibly applied to the image.
task_completion.visual_evidence: The image on the canvas shows a solarization effect where bright areas are darkened/inverted, but dark areas remain relatively normal. It should look distinct from a simple full-color inversion (negative).

task_completion.description: The modified image is saved or exported.
task_completion.visual_evidence: A 'Save', 'Save As', or 'Export' dialog is visible and being confirmed, or the application title bar shows the file is saved (e.g., no unsaved asterisk next to the filename).

integrity.description: The agent used the graphical user interface of an image editor to apply the effect.
integrity.visual_evidence: Screenshots show interaction with an image editor's GUI (like GIMP) rather than terminal commands (e.g., ImageMagick) to process the image.

integrity.description: The effect applied is a genuine solarization, not a simple color inversion.
integrity.visual_evidence: The resulting image must show partial inversion (brights darkened, darks preserved), not a complete negative where all colors and brightness levels are simply inverted.

privileged_info_for_vlm: The task requires applying a solarization effect to a landscape image (originally 1000x600 pixels). A true solarization effect (such as GIMP's Solarize filter at the default threshold of 127) partially inverts the image: bright regions are darkened (inverted), while dark regions are preserved. The final image must NOT be a simple complete negative/inversion. The verifier checks for >30% darkening in bright areas and <20% change in dark areas.
